# Study design {#sec-studydesign}

The 
[Background section](#sec-background)
laid out the motivation for this study.
In short, prior uncertainty about the timing of events (divergence times or
population size changes) across taxa can create a strong penalty against
comparative models with more event time parameters, leading to a downward bias
in the number of evolutionary events shared across taxa (i.e., overclustering
of taxa to a smaller number of events).
In this study, we will use simualations to determine if a hiearchical Bayesian
approach---specifically hyperpriors on the parameters of the prior on event
times---can mitigate this bias.

We will focus on divergence times in this study and will use that language
below, rather than the more abstract "evolutionary event times".

## Goals

Using simulated comparative biogeographic data, we aim to:

1.  [Demonstrate the downward bias on the number of divergence events created
    by prior uncertainty about the timing of events.]{#goal-demo-bias}
2.  [Assess if hyperpriors over the timing of divergence events mitigate the
    bias.]{#goal-assess-hp}

Below we expand on how we designed the study to address each goal.

## Demonstrating bias imposed by vague divergence-time priors

TODO

## Assess hyperprior solution to bias

TODO
